mnemonics: COW: Don't milk it until you need it. Only create a copy when a process tries to write (milk) the shared resource.
Think of a library book (shared page). Everyone can read it. Only when you want to write in the margins (modify it) do you have to get your own photocopy (private copy).
keyPoints: A resource management technique that delays copying a shared resource until the first write operation.
Promotes efficiency by allowing processes to share the same physical memory pages initially.
Only when a process modifies the data is a private copy created, making the modification isolated to that process.
commonMistakes: Confusing COW with simply sharing memory. The key differentiator is the deferred copy on write-access.
Assuming COW is only for forking. While pivotal there, it's also used in other areas (e.g., memory-mapped files).
Overestimating the performance benefit if most forked processes immediately write to all pages, causing the overhead of numerous copies.
